text.title: Notion Database Design Rules
text.summary: Rules for relational database design, property type selection, rollup patterns, and performance constraints in Notion.

rule.statement: Create three core databases: Projects (one row per initiative), Tasks (one row per work item), Sprints (one row per iteration). Connect them bidirectionally: Tasks relation to Projects (and back), Tasks relation to Sprints (and back). This schema supports rollups for completion tracking without duplicating data.
rule.rationale: Flat databases require manual status tracking. Relations + rollups compute project progress, sprint velocity, and workload automatically from task data.

rule.statement: Limit database properties to 15-20 maximum per database. Archive properties that are not used in views, rollups, or automations.
rule.rationale: Notion databases with many properties load slowly. Complex formulas referencing many relations are the primary performance bottleneck.

rule.statement: Use Status property type (not Select) for task workflow states. Status groups Not Started / In Progress / Complete and renders progress rings in relation views — Select does not.
rule.rationale: The Status type is purpose-built for workflow tracking and enables native progress visualization that Select cannot replicate.

rule.statement: Use Relation + Rollup for computed metrics (task count per project, total story points, completion %). Never compute these manually in a formula that re-reads source database rows — rollups are cached; formulas that iterate rows are not.
rule.rationale: Formulas that aggregate related rows query the source database on every page load. Rollups are pre-computed and significantly faster on large databases.

example.note: Project "Completed Tasks": Rollup → relation: Tasks → property: Status → calculation: Count values filtered to "Done". Project "Progress %": Formula → round(prop("Completed Tasks") / prop("Total Tasks") * 100). Project "Total Points": Rollup → relation: Tasks → property: Estimate → calculation: Sum.

antipattern.description: Formula that uses prop("Tasks") to iterate task relations and check each task's status manually.
antipattern.reason: Formula iteration over relations is not supported in Notion formulas. Use a Rollup with a filter instead.

rule.statement: Archive completed sprints and done tasks older than 90 days to an Archive database. Use linked databases (not duplicated databases) to show archived data in context when needed.
rule.rationale: Notion databases with 500+ active rows and complex formula properties load in 3-8 seconds. Archiving old data keeps the primary database fast.
